resume:
ALLISON BRENNAN
HR Coordinator / Junior People Operations Specialist
Denver, CO | allison.brennan.hr@email.com | open to hybrid

PROFILE
Early-career people-operations professional with about two and a half years of generalist HR experience at a mid-sized technology company. I started in a recruiting-coordinator seat and grew into a broader people-operations role covering onboarding, HRIS administration, benefits support, and employee-relations intake. I describe myself as the person who keeps the unglamorous machinery running so that employees never have to think about it. I am most confident in the operational, process, and data-hygiene side of HR. I am still developing my judgment on the harder, more ambiguous employee-relations and policy questions, and I am explicit about that because I would rather escalate early than guess on something that affects a person's livelihood.

EXPERIENCE

People Operations Specialist — Lumen Tide Software (about 600 employees), March 2024 to Present
Lumen Tide is a B2B software company that roughly doubled headcount during my time here, which meant the people team was constantly trying to build process while the plane was in the air. I report to the Senior People Operations Manager and partner closely with recruiting, finance (for payroll), and IT (for provisioning).
- Own the end-to-end onboarding workflow for all new hires, from offer-acceptance to day-one readiness. I coordinate background checks, I-9 verification, equipment provisioning with IT, and the first-week schedule. When I took it over the process lived in one person's head and a half-broken spreadsheet; I rebuilt it into a documented checklist in our HRIS so any coordinator could run it.
- Administer the HRIS (Rippling) day to day: I process new hires, terminations, role changes, and compensation updates, and I am the person who notices when a field is wrong before it flows into payroll. I cleaned up a backlog of mismatched manager-assignment records that had been quietly breaking the org chart and some approval routing.
- Run benefits-enrollment support during open enrollment, fielding the high volume of employee questions, escalating the genuinely complicated ones to our benefits broker, and tracking completion so nobody missed the window.
- Serve as first-line intake for employee-relations questions. I do not adjudicate the serious ones — I triage, document the initial report carefully, and route to my manager or to legal depending on severity. I have learned to be very careful about what I write down and who I loop in.
- Pull recurring people-data reports for leadership: headcount, attrition, time-to-fill, and onboarding-completion rates. I am the keeper of the numbers the people team reports up, which has made me almost obsessive about data hygiene because a wrong headcount number is embarrassing in front of the exec team.
- Helped coordinate a small reduction-in-force from the logistics side — preparing accurate documentation, sequencing system access changes, and making sure offboarding was handled with dignity. I was not a decision-maker on who was affected; I owned the operational execution and the paperwork accuracy.
- Maintain the employee handbook and policy acknowledgment tracking, chasing down the stragglers who never sign and keeping a clean record of who acknowledged which version of which policy, because that record matters the moment anything is ever disputed.
- Built and maintain the onboarding-completion dashboard that the people team reviews weekly, so a new hire never falls through the cracks between offer and day one. Before this we relied on someone remembering, which is exactly the kind of process that works until the one time it does not.

WHAT I HAVE LEARNED ABOUT THE WORK
Most of my growth in this role has been less about new tools and more about judgment in small, repeated moments. A few things I have internalized: First, in HR the cost of a sloppy record is not paid by me, it is paid later by an employee or by the company in a dispute, so I treat documentation as a form of care rather than bureaucracy. Second, the operational layer of HR is where trust is either built or quietly destroyed — if a new hire's laptop is not ready on day one, no amount of warm onboarding language repairs that first impression. Third, I have learned to say the words I do not know, let me find out rather than improvise an answer on benefits or policy, because a confident wrong answer in HR travels fast and is hard to walk back. Fourth, I have learned to read the difference between an employee who wants to vent and an employee who is reporting something that has to move up the chain immediately, and I would still rather escalate one too many than one too few. I see all of this as the foundation I am building before I take on the harder, more ambiguous people-partner work where the right answer is genuinely unclear and the stakes for a person are real.

Recruiting Coordinator — Lumen Tide Software, October 2023 to March 2024
- Scheduled interviews across multiple time zones and managed candidate communication in the ATS (Greenhouse).
- Built a simple interviewer-load tracker so we stopped overbooking the same three engineers, which improved candidate scheduling turnaround.
- This is where I learned that the candidate experience is mostly an operations problem dressed up as a relationship problem.

HR Intern — Front Range Health Partners, Summer 2023
- Supported the HR team with filing, data entry, and new-hire paperwork.
- Sat in on (but did not lead) new-hire orientations and learned the basics of compliance documentation.

EDUCATION
B.A. Organizational Psychology, Colorado State University, 2023
Relevant coursework: organizational behavior, employment law basics, statistics for social science.

CERTIFICATIONS & SKILLS
SHRM-CP (in progress, exam scheduled). HRIS: Rippling (strong), Workday (exposure). ATS: Greenhouse. Tools: Excel (advanced — pivot tables, VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP, basic data cleaning), Google Workspace. Strengths: process documentation, data hygiene, onboarding logistics, careful employee-relations intake and escalation, discretion with confidential information.

WHAT I AM WORKING ON
I want to grow from the operational layer into real people-partner judgment — handling ambiguous employee-relations cases with more independence, advising managers on performance conversations, and contributing to policy rather than only executing it. I learn best with a strong manager who lets me sit in on the hard conversations before I have to lead them. I am honest about the edge of my experience because in HR the cost of pretending to know something you don't is borne by a real employee.
jd: Brightwater Labs is hiring a Junior People Operations Specialist to support a fast-growing 700-person organization. Reporting to the Head of People, you will own onboarding and offboarding logistics, HRIS data integrity, benefits-enrollment support, and first-line employee-relations intake. We are looking for someone operationally excellent and unusually careful with sensitive information — the kind of person who documents precisely and knows exactly when to escalate rather than improvise. Core responsibilities: run the new-hire workflow end to end, keep the HRIS clean enough that leadership reporting is trustworthy, support open enrollment, and triage employee concerns to the right owner. The ideal candidate has 1-3 years of HR-coordinator or people-ops experience, strong systems and spreadsheet skills, and sound instincts about confidentiality and escalation. We will probe how you handle ambiguous or sensitive situations and will look for clear, structured thinking under a little pressure.

history.q: Tell me about your current people-ops role and who you partner with day to day.
You rebuilt the onboarding process. What specifically was broken before?
How do you know the new checklist actually improved anything — any numbers?
You mention an HRIS data cleanup. What was wrong with the manager-assignment records?
How did bad manager records break approval routing in practice?
Let's talk employee relations. How do you decide what to escalate versus handle yourself?
Walk me through your documentation habit on an intake — what exactly do you write down?
Tell me about open enrollment. What was the hardest part operationally?
You helped with a reduction in force. What exactly was your role?
How did you keep the offboarding dignified on the logistics side?
What's a people-data report you own, and how do you keep it accurate?
Describe a time you caught a data error before it hit payroll.
Now the harder one: tell me about a sensitive employee-relations situation where you had to make a judgment call about confidentiality in the moment.
Who did you tell, and who did you deliberately not tell?
Walk me through that one situation step by step — what you were told, what you wrote down, exactly who you looped in, and how you decided the boundary of what to share.

candidate_last_answer: Right, okay, so — this is a good question and honestly a hard one to talk about because, you know, confidentiality, so I want to be careful even describing it. Um. So there was a situation, an employee came to me — this was, I want to say, last fall, maybe? — and they shared something that was, you know, pretty sensitive, about a manager. And I remember thinking, oh, this is above my pay grade. Sorry, let me back up, I think context matters here — so part of why I was even the first stop is that I sit at the intake point, which I mentioned earlier. Anyway. So they told me this thing, and I — okay so the thing about these situations is you don't want to overreact but you also don't want to sit on it, right? So I think I told my manager. I'm fairly sure I looped her in pretty quickly. And I wrote some of it down, the gist of it, though honestly in the moment I was a little flustered because the person was upset and I was trying to just, like, be present for them. There might have been a question of whether legal needed to be in it, and I think eventually they were, or my manager handled that part. I don't think I told anyone I shouldn't have — I'm pretty careful about that. Sorry, this is rambly. What specifically did you want — the writing-down part, or the who-I-told part?

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I think I told my manager. I'm fairly sure I looped her in pretty quickly

expected-yield: a precise, de-rambled sequence — what she actually documented versus left out, the exact timing and recipient of the escalation, and the explicit reasoning by which she drew the confidentiality boundary, replacing the hedged 'I think / I'm fairly sure' with a concrete account
